paper_title: Tooth Isotope Analysis and Isoscape Modeling

methods.description: Criteria for selecting M3 teeth, demographic profile, and initial cleaning/sterilization steps applied to samples used in the study before specific isotopic analyses.

steps.description: Before collecting and using samples from live subjects, appropriate permissions were obtained. (Documentation may be found in Regan [3].)
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain necessary permissions for using human samples.

steps.description: Only M3 (crown completion circa 1996-1999; root completion 1996-2005) extracted during the winter of 2005/6 from USAFA cadets born in 1984 ± 1 (1 s) were used in this study. Two outliers born in 1979 were included in the Pb study for statistical comparison. Thus, dentin remodelling was negligible.
steps.searchable_summary: Select M3 teeth samples based on specific criteria (extraction year, birth year, tooth development stage).
inputs: Database of available tooth samples with associated metadata (extraction date, birth date, tooth type, development stage)

steps.description: Molars used to develop the isoscapes in this study were from individuals who self-reported living in a single location from their year of birth until after 2000.
steps.searchable_summary: Filter selected tooth samples based on individual's self-reported residential history (single location).
outputs: Subset of tooth samples for isoscape development

steps.description: The sex and race/ethnicity demographic profile of the samples used for the isoscapes were based on self-identification: 80% male, 20% female, 88% White, 6% Hispanic (any race), 4.5% Black/African and 1.5% Asian [3].
steps.searchable_summary: Document demographic profile of samples used for isoscapes.
inputs: Subset of tooth samples for isoscape development

steps.description: All teeth used in Regan's [3,4] and this study were first cleaned of organic matter and sterilized by soaking the teeth in 3% H 2 O 2 for 2 days.
steps.searchable_summary: Clean and sterilize tooth samples by soaking in hydrogen peroxide.

steps.description: Any adherent periodontal tissue was also removed.
steps.searchable_summary: Manually remove periodontal tissue from tooth samples.
inputs: Cleaned tooth samples with adherent tissue
outputs: Cleaned tooth samples without adherent tissue

steps.description: After sonicating in 18 MX water for 30 min and subsequent drying, interior enamel powder was obtained using a carbide drill bit.
steps.searchable_summary: Sonicate tooth samples in high-purity water, dry, and obtain interior enamel powder using a drill bit.

steps.description: The powders were subsequently treated with 30% H 2 O 2 to remove organic residues and dilute acetic acid to remove any secondary carbonates before analysis [3].
steps.searchable_summary: Treat enamel powder with hydrogen peroxide and acetic acid to remove organic residues and secondary carbonates.

methods.description: Procedure for preparing and analyzing enamel powder for Pb isotopes using Dowex columns and MC-ICP-MS, as described in Regan's study [3].

steps.description: In Regan's study, the enamel powder analysed for Pb was dissolved overnight in 8 mol/L HNO 3 (triply distilled) in Teflon vials and taken to dryness.
steps.searchable_summary: Dissolve enamel powder in nitric acid and evaporate to dryness.

steps.description: Pb bromide was formed by dissolving the residue with 1 mol/L HBr (Optima) and separated in a class-1000 clean lab using Dowex 1X-8 micro-columns following the method of Manhes et al. [39].
steps.searchable_summary: Form lead bromide and separate lead using anion exchange chromatography (Dowex 1X-8 columns).

steps.description: Analyses of the Pb isotope ratios were conducted using a Nu-Plasma, Multi-Collector Inductively-Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometer (MC-ICP-MS).
steps.searchable_summary: Measure lead isotope ratios using MC-ICP-MS.

steps.description: Both this study and Regan's [3] included analyses of laboratory blanks and NIST standards.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze laboratory blanks and NIST standards for quality control.

methods.description: Procedure for measuring structural carbonate d18O in enamel powder using IRMS, as described in Regan's study [3].

steps.description: The enamel's structural carbonate d 18 O c was measured in the enamel powder using a VG/Micromass PRISM Series II isotope ratio mass spectrometer (IRMS) with 90 C H 3 PO 4 in an automatic Isocarb preparation device. (The H 3 PO 4 is used to convert the enamel carbonates to CO 2(g) . Note that different acid temperatures can create measurable isotope fractionations [40].)
steps.searchable_summary: Measure structural carbonate d18O in enamel powder using IRMS with phosphoric acid digestion.

methods.method_name: Pb Concentration and Isotope Analysis (This Study's Method)
methods.description: Procedure for preparing and analyzing enamel and dentin samples for Pb concentration and isotopes using Eichrom Pb resin and ICP-MS/MC-ICP-MS, based on Keller et al. [4].

steps.description: In this study, tooth surfaces were cleaned with a carbide burr and sonication in 18 MX H 2 O that removed any remaining surface contamination and tissues.
steps.searchable_summary: Clean tooth surfaces using a carbide burr and sonication in high-purity water.

steps.description: Crown enamel and mid-root dentin samples (10-20 mg) were removed inside of virgin polypropylene (PP) bags to avoid contamination.
steps.searchable_summary: Sample crown enamel and mid-root dentin (10-20 mg) inside clean bags to prevent contamination.

steps.description: Dentin and enamel were manually selected under magnification.
steps.searchable_summary: Manually select enamel and dentin material under magnification.

steps.description: Following the method in Bower et al. [41] and Keller et al. [4], these samples were dissolved overnight in 1.0 mL of 16 mol/L triply distilled HNO 3 in capped, virgin PP vials on an orbital shaker at 200 rpm.
steps.searchable_summary: Dissolve enamel and dentin samples overnight in nitric acid in PP vials on an orbital shaker.

steps.description: The solutions were diluted to 5.0 mL with 18 MX H 2 O, 20 mg of Eichrom Pb resin was added and the mixture was placed on an orbital shaker at 200 rpm for 1 h.
steps.searchable_summary: Dilute sample solutions, add Eichrom Pb resin, and mix on an orbital shaker.

steps.description: Cleaning of the resin-bound Pb was performed by capturing the resin in acid-washed and rinsed PTFE columns followed by 3.0 mL of 2.0 mol/L HNO 3 .
steps.searchable_summary: Capture resin in columns and clean resin-bound lead with nitric acid wash.

steps.description: Extraction of the Pb was achieved with 3.0 mL 18 MX H 2 O and 1.0 mL 0.050 mol/L ammonium oxalate (Sigma, 0.01% trace metals) collected in PP vials which had 1.0 mL of 2.0 mol/L HNO 3 added to avoid subsequent Pb precipitation.
steps.searchable_summary: Elute lead from resin using high-purity water and ammonium oxalate, collecting in PP vials with added nitric acid.

steps.description: The Pb solutions were analysed for their Pb concentrations using a Thermo-Fisher iCAP Q ICP-MS. The data were collected with an instrumental precision of about 5% and a detection limit of 0.03 ppb.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure lead concentrations in purified solutions using ICP-MS.

steps.description: Pb isotope ratios were obtained using a Nu-Plasma High Resolution MC-ICP-MS.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure lead isotope ratios using High Resolution MC-ICP-MS.

steps.description: A Tl spike used for mass-bias correction was added to each sample just before analysis [33,42] and sample bracketing with NIST-981 was used to correct for any instrument drift.
steps.searchable_summary: Add thallium spike for mass bias correction and use sample bracketing with NIST-981 standard for instrument drift correction during MC-ICP-MS analysis.

steps.description: Both this study and Regan's [3] included analyses of laboratory blanks and NIST standards.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze laboratory blanks and NIST standards for quality control.

methods.description: Procedures for developing and using isoscapes, modeling temperature effects on d18O, and creating a d18O difference isoscape based on climate data and isotopic fractionation principles.

steps.description: We predicted temperature fluctuations over time would create deviations from the measured 1996 d 18 O c isoscape. These Dd 18 O c offsets before and after 1996 were modelled using historic records of annual temperature anomalies [22].
steps.searchable_summary: Model deviations in d18Oc values over time based on historic annual temperature anomalies.

steps.description: These were used to estimate the magnitude and sign that changing locations should have on an individual's enamel d 18 O c .
steps.searchable_summary: Estimate the effect of changing geographic location on individual enamel d18Oc based on modeled temperature effects.
outputs: Estimated d18Oc changes due to relocation

steps.description: These temperature changes were also used to calculate the offset expected between the median time of crown formation (1996) and mid-root formation (2002) [28].
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate the expected d18O offset between tooth crown (1996) and root (2002) formation times based on temperature changes.
outputs: Expected d18O offset between crown and root

steps.description: To create an isoscape of these differences, the D C anomalies for the NOAA/NCEI US Climate Divisions were used to estimate corresponding Dd 18 O c values by subtracting the Bayesian-weighted data for 1996 from the data for 2002 in each climate division [43]) and then using Equations (3) and (4) (see below) to convert D C to Dd 18 O c(VPDB) .
steps.searchable_summary: Create a d18Oc difference isoscape by calculating temperature anomalies between 2002 and 1996 for climate divisions, applying Bayesian weighting, and converting temperature differences to d18Oc differences using specified equations.
inputs: NOAA/NCEI US Climate Division temperature anomaly data (1996, 2002)

steps.description: For simplicity, the d 18 O w to d 18 O c conversions were assumed the same for enamel and dentin and the Bayesian weights were assumed to follow a 5-year square distribution centred on 1996 and 2002, respectively. Because mineralisation occurs in episodic cycles that span multiple years, a square distribution was thought to provide as realistic a weighting of the fluctuations as a Gaussian distribution.
steps.searchable_summary: Define assumptions for d18O conversion between water and carbonate and specify Bayesian weighting distribution for isoscape calculation.
outputs: Defined model assumptions (d18Ow-d18Oc conversion equality, square Bayesian weighting distribution)

steps.description: Using global mid-latitude data, Fricke and O'Neil [45] obtained an a ¼ 0.578 for the winter and 0.417 for the summer. Liu et al. [46] measured a winter a ¼ 0.64 and a summer a ¼ 0.38 for 1992 and 0.63 and 0.56, respectively, for 1993 for the USA. Dansgaard [32] gave a number of different values for a around 20 C ranging from 0.24 to 0.78 d 18 O&/ C for various conditions. Thus, þ0.5 d 18 O&/ C was used for estimating the responsivity of d 18 O w(VSMOW) to temperature in Equation (3), as our method of sampling the enamel and dentin included multiple years that average the a extremes.
steps.searchable_summary: Select a specific value (+0.5 d18O/C) for the temperature sensitivity parameter (alpha) in the d18O-temperature relationship (Equation 3) based on literature review and sample characteristics.
